text: Hello everyone, this is Roxan Liu back with a robotic tap left lower quadrant incisional hernia repair. I'm calling it a spagalian hernia but it was actually more of an interstitial hernia above the arcuate line. laterally. The patient also has a recurrent inguinal hernia. His past surgical history is significant for a repair of an undescended left testes through a long left lower quadrant incision. And I believe this hernia that you see down at the bottom of the screen is from that. So it's not a true Spigalian, but maybe some type of incisional hernia.

The surgeon performs preperitoneal dissection, peeling the areolar tissue from the abdominal wall to reveal a shiny fascial layer. The thin peritoneum is visible, while the robust areolar tissue is stripped off, resembling white cotton candy. As the dissection progresses laterally, the preperitoneal plane becomes easier to develop, with increased fat visible.

The surgeon uses the left hand for retraction to reveal the dissection plane around the hernia sac. The sac is reduced, showing a true hernia sac that is easily manipulated. The surgeon aims for a mesh overlap of five to six centimeters, creating a large pocket in the groin area while staying on the peritoneum and leaving fat against the abdominal wall.

The surgeon uses scissors as a hot scalpel to score tissue layers, providing hemostatic dissection aided by left-hand retraction. The operation progresses past the hernia, revealing normal tissue planes. The surgeon approaches the peritoneal side for more overlap, exposing a small hernia with fatty tissue protruding through the abdominal wall. Inferior epigastric vessels are visible to the right, and the dissection continues towards the testicular vessels in the groin area, maintaining proximity to the peritoneum while leaving extraperitoneal fat against the abdominal wall.

The surgeon dissects medially, skeletonizing the vas deferens while performing step five of the critical view of the malpector and the orifice. Good retraction and hemostasis facilitate the procedure, allowing the surgeon to cut through the fat on the superior pubic rami and pubic tubercle to expose Cooper's ligament.

The surgeon follows the areolar tissue trail to guide the dissection, completing step five of the procedure. The anatomical review reveals an interstitial hernia above the arcuate line, with visible testicular vessels and the vas deferens in the vas wall. The internal ring is skeletonized, and the infra-epigastric vessels are dissected in the retropubic space, extending nearly two centimeters deep towards the obturator space. The surgeon prepares to close the defect.

The surgeon performs additional dissection before closing the defect using an Ovilac 180 suture. The suture is applied to the superficial layers, likely the external oblique, to obliterate dead space and prevent seroma formation. The surgeon efficiently manages the needle, ensuring it does not get caught and uses both hands to tighten the suture.

The surgeon runs the suture back a few throws to lock it in place. They measure the area to ensure a 16x12 Progrip mesh provides adequate coverage over the inguinal space and interstitial hernia. The mesh is secured with 3-0 Vicryl sutures to maintain its position as the pockets evacuate and the patient moves postoperatively.

The surgeon sets the lower flap of the ProGrip mesh first, then unfolds the upper flap to cover the interstitial hernia, indirect space, direct space, and femoral space. The mesh is secured with three stitches to prevent shifting, especially laterally where it may not adhere well to the shiny transversalis fascia. The flap is closed using a six-inch 3-0 V-lock suture, with the left hand aiding in efficient needle handling.
